questionText:
Which of the following statements with reference to Akshay Kumar Dutta is/are correct?
1. He brought medical opinion to support his views against child marriage.
2. He declared that “rationalism is our only preceptor’’.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Akshay Kumar Dutta was a famous Bengali writer who was amongst the pioneers of the Bengal Renaissance. He was born in the area of Chupi, Bardhaman, in the state of West Bengal, in the year 1820.
● He declared that “rationalism is our only preceptor’’, and held that all natural and social phenomena could be analysed and understood by purely mechanical processes. He thus used a rational approach to study tradition; he evaluated the contemporary socio-religious practices from the standpoint of social utility and replaced faith with rationality. Thus, he repudiated the infallibility of the Vedas. So, statement 2 is correct.
● He also brought medical opinion to support his views against child marriage. Akshay Kumar Datta associated himself with the Tattvabodhini Sabha and was appointed the assistant secretary in 1839. The next year, he was offered the designation of a teacher of the Tattwabodhini Pathsala, which he accepted. In 1843, Akshay Kumar Datta's 'Tattwabodhini Patrika' was published. So, statement 1 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Widow Remarriage Association:
1. It was founded by Vishnu Shastri Pandit in Maharashtra.
2. Dhondo Keshav Karve was associated as the secretary of the Association.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Due to the efforts of Pandit Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar (1820-91), the principal of Sanskrit College, Calcutta,that the Hindu Widows’ Remarriage Act, 1856, was passed; it legalised the marriage of widows and declared issues from such marriages as legitimate. In Maharashtra, Vishnu Shastri Pandit founded the Widow Remarriage Association in 1850. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Efforts were also made by Professor Dhondo Keshav Karve in western India to promote Widow remarriage. Karve himself married a widow in 1893. He dedicated his life to the upliftment of Hindu widows and became the secretary of the Widow Remarriage Association. He opened a widows’ home in Poona to give the high caste widows an interest in life by providing them with facilities for vocational training. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Subramaniyam Tirumambu, A.K. Gopalan and P. Krishna Pillai were actively associated with which one of the following?
options: b) Temple entry movement in Guruvayur
d) Civil disobedience movement in Andhra
explanation:
● In 1931, when the civil disobedience movement was suspended, a temple entry movement was organised in Kerala. Inspired by K. Kelappan, poet Subramaniyam Tirumambu (the ‘singing sword of Kerala’) led a group of sixteen volunteers to Guruvayur. Leaders like P. Krishna Pillai and A.K. Gopalan were among the satyagrahis.
● Finally, on November 12, 1936, the Maharaja of Travancore issued a proclamation throwing open all government-controlled temples to all Hindus. A similar step was taken by the C. Rajagopalachari administration in Madras in 1938.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Theosophical Society:
1. It was founded by Annie Besant in Adyar.
2. It was inspired from the philosophy of Upanishads and believed in reincarnation and karma.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?
explanation:
● A group of westerners led by Madame H.P. Blavatsky (1831- 1891) and Colonel M.S. Olcott, who was inspired by Indian thought and culture, founded the Theosophical Society in New York City, United States in 1875. In 1882, they shifted their headquarters to Adyar, on the outskirts of Madras (at that time) in India. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● Society believed that a special relationship could be established between a person’s soul and God by contemplation, prayer, revelation, etc. It accepted the Hindu beliefs in reincarnation and karma and drew inspiration from the philosophy of the Upanishads and Samkhya, yoga and Vedanta schools of thought. It aimed to work for a universal brotherhood of humanity without distinction of race, creed, sex, caste or colour. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Swami Vivekananda:
1. He was the founder of the Ramakrishna Math.
2. He called for a blend of the materialism of the West and the spiritualism of the East.
3. He advocated the message “the service of jiva (living objects) is the worship of Siva”.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
● Ramakrishna Paramahamsa laid the foundations of the Ramakrishna Math with his young monastic disciples as a nucleus to bring into existence a band of monks dedicated to a life of renunciation and practical spirituality, from among whom teachers and workers would be sent out to spread the universal message of Vedanta. Swami Vivekananda, after Ramakrishna’s death, founded the Ramakrishna Mission in 1897. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● Swami Vivekananda advocated for a healthy balance between spiritualism and materialism. Envisaging a new culture for the whole world, he called for a blend of the materialism of the West and the spiritualism of the East into a new harmony to produce happiness for mankind. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Vivekananda was a great humanist and used the Ramakrishna Mission for humanitarian relief and social work. The Mission stands for religious and social reform. Vivekananda advocated the doctrine of service—the service of all beings. The service of jiva (living objects) is the worship of Siva. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl.No. Work Author
1. Amrita Bazar Patrika : Madam Bhikaji Cama
2. Bahishkrit Bharat : B.R. Ambedkar
3. Bande Mataram : Sisir Kumar Ghosh
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
● Amrita Bazar Patrika was first published in 1868 by Sisir Kumar Ghosh and Moti Lal Ghosh in Bengal. So, pair 1 is not correctly matched.
● MookNayak (1920) and Bahishkrit Bharat (1927) were two Marathi journals edited by Dr. B. R. Ambedkar. So, pair 2 is correctly matched.
● Bande Mataram was founded by Madam Bhikaji Cama. The paper along with the later publication of Talvar was aimed at inciting nationalist unrest in India. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Manav Dharma Sabha:
1. It was founded by Radhakant Deb.
2. It challenged irrational superstitions prevalent in Hinduism.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Manav Dharma Sabha was one of the most prominent and influential organisations that motivated the Hindu reform movements in Surat and all over Gujarat as well. The Sabha was founded in 1844 by Mehtaji Durgaram Manchharam in Surat. Durgaram had companions like Dadoba Panderung Tarkhad, Dinmani Shankar, Dalpatram Bhagubai, Damodar Das, etc. in the group. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● The Manav Dharma Sabha challenged irrational superstitions prevalent in Hinduism. It also challenged the magicians and the reciters of incantations to demonstrate their skills. The Sabha also announced prize money for anybody who could do it successfully. However, though they criticized the Caste system, they did not take any direct action against this institution. Durgaram and his friends rejected the existence of ghosts, their exorcism using incantations. The ideas of the Manav Dharma Sabha were passed on to the later influential organisations like the Paramahansa Mandali in Maharashtra. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Mahad Satyagraha was led by E. V. Ramasamy to challenge the regressive customs of caste discrimination.
2. The ‘Bole Resolution’ of 1923 called for the opening up of public water reservoirs to untouchables.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar led the Mahad Satyagraha in March 1927 to challenge the regressive customs of the caste Hindus. He stressed the necessity of removing ideas of ‘high’ and ‘low’ and inculcating self-elevation through self-help, self-respect and self-knowledge. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● Bombay Legislative Council had adopted a resolution moved by S.K. Bole on August 4, 1923. The Bole Resolution said, “The Council recommends the Untouchable Classes be allowed to use all public watering places, wells and Dharamshala's which are built and maintained out of public funds or administered by bodies appointed by Government or created by statute, as well as public schools, courts, offices and dispensaries”. However, the resolution remained on paper until Dr. Ambedkar and his companions went to drink water from the Chavdar tank of Mahad on March 20, 1927. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Later in December 1927, Ambedkar and his colleagues burnt the ‘Manusmriti’ at the same place as a gesture of getting rid of inequalities.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Widows’ Remarriage Act, 1856 legalised the marriage of widows.
2. The Sarda Act of 1930 forbade the marriage of girls below the age of 12.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● It was mainly due to the efforts of Pandit Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar (1820-91), the principal of Sanskrit College, Calcutta, that the Hindu Widows’ Remarriage Act, 1856, was passed. It legalised the marriage of widows and declared issues from such marriages as legitimate. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The relentless efforts of a Parsi reformer, B.M. Malabari, were rewarded by the enactment of the Age of Consent Act (1891) which forbade the marriage of girls below the age of 12. Later, the Sarda Act (1930) further pushed up the marriage age to 18 and 14 for boys and girls, respectively. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Raja Ram Mohan Roy:
1. He started ‘Atmiya Sabha’ to propagate the monotheistic ideals of the Vedanta.
2. He supported David Hare to establish the Hindu College in 1817.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● In 1814, Raja Ram Mohan Roy started the Atmiya Sabha (Society of Friends), to nurture philosophical discussions on the idea of monotheism in Vedanta and to campaign against idolatry, casteism, child marriage and other social ills. The Atmiya Sabha would make way for the Brahmo Sabha in 1828, set up with Debendranath Tagore, Rabindranath Tagore’s father. So, statement 1 is correct.
● He applied the principles of rationalism to other sects also, particularly to the elements of blind faith in them. In his Precepts of Jesus (1820), he tried to separate the moral and philosophical message of the New Testament, which he praised, from its miracle stories.
● Ram Mohan Roy did much to disseminate the benefits of modern education to his countrymen. He supported David Hare’s efforts to establish the Hindu College in 1817, while Roy’s English school taught mechanics and Voltaire’s philosophy. In 1825, he established a Vedanta college where courses in both Indian learning and Western social and physical sciences were offered. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Prarthana Samaj:
1. Its leaders included Atmaram Pandurang, R.G. Bhandarkar and N.G. Chandavarkar.
2. It mainly relied on education and persuasion and not on confrontation with Hindu orthodoxy.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● In 1867, Keshab Chandra Sen helped Atmaram Pandurang found the Prarthana Samaj in Bombay. Mahadeo Govind Ranade joined the samaj in 1870 and much of the popularity of and work done by the society was due to his efforts. Other leaders of the samaj were R.G. Bhandarkar and N.G. Chandavarkar. The samaj relied on education and persuasion and not on confrontation with Hindu orthodoxy. There was a four-point social agenda: disapproval of the caste system, women’s education, widow remarriage, and raising the age of marriage for both males and females. So, both statements 1 and 2 are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following personalities wrote ‘‘Barnaparichay’’, a primer for missionary schools?
explanation:
● Pandit Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar (1820-91) was a great scholar and reformer. Vidyasagar’s ideas were a happy blend of Indian and Western thought. In 1850, he became the principal of Sanskrit College. He was determined to break the priestly monopoly of scriptural knowledge, and for this he opened the Sanskrit College to non-brahmins. He introduced Western thought in Sanskrit College to break the self-imposed isolation of Sanskritic learning.
● He chose Indian languages as vehicles of reform and to enable vernacular to perform this role successfully, he prepared language primers. Barnaparichay or Barna Parichay (1855) is a Bengali primer written by Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar. It is "the most influential primer of Bengal" which became the template for all other primers and the most used primer even in missionary schools.
● Other prominent literary works of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar include books such as Bahubibaha and Bidhaba Bibaha. He also started the Bengali newspaper Shome Prakash in the year 1858.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Age of Consent Act (1891), consider the following statements:
1. Sir Andrew Scoble was associated with the enactment of the Act.
2. Bal Gangadhar Tilak opposed the Act as it interfered with orthodox Hindu codes.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Age of Consent Act, 1891, also known as Act X of 1891 was legislation enacted by the British government to raise the age of consent for sexual intercourse for all girls, married or unmarried, from ten to twelve years in all jurisdictions.
● It was the death of an eleven-year-old Bengali girl Phulmoni Dasi due to forceful intercourse by her 35-year-old husband in 1889 that led the British to pass the Act in 1891. Sir Andrew Scoble introduced the Act in the legislative council of Governor General of India in Calcutta. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Sir Romesh Chunder Mitter opposed the Act on the grounds that it interfered with orthodox Hindu codes. Bal Gangadhar Tilak stated that “We would not like that the government should have anything to do with regulating our social customs or ways of living, even supposing that the act of government will be a very beneficial and suitable measure.” So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the ‘Servants of India Society’:
1. It was founded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale.
2. It chose to remain aloof from political activities and organisations.
3. It aimed to to prepare a cadre of selfless workers who were to devote their lives to the cause of the country in a religious spirit.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Gopal Krishna Gokhale (1866-1915), a liberal leader of the Indian National Congress, founded the Servants of India Society in 1905 with the help of M.G. Ranade. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The Servants of India Society chose to remain aloof from political activities and organisations like the Indian National Congress. So, statement 2 is correct.
● The society aimed to train national missionaries for the service of India; to promote, by all constitutional means, the true interests of the Indian people; and to prepare a cadre of selfless workers who were to devote their lives to the cause of the country in a religious spirit. In 1911, the Hitavada began to be published to project the views of society. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Pandita Ramabai:
1. She pleaded for improvement in the educational syllabus of Indian women before the English Education Commission.
2. Her book ‘The High Caste Hindu Woman’ analyzes the ‘code of Manu’ which prescribes the rules for life of a high caste Hindu woman.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Pandita Ramabai Saraswati founded the Arya Mahila Samaj to serve the cause of women. She pleaded for improvement in the educational syllabus of Indian women before the English Education Commission which was referred to Queen Victoria. Later Ramabai Ranade established a branch of Arya Mahila Samaj in Bombay. Ramabai Ranade founded the Ladies Social Conference (Bharat Mahila Parishad), under the parent organisation National Social Conference, in 1904 in Bombay. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Pandita Ramabai’s landmark book ‘The High Caste Hindu Woman’ was published in 1887. Its seven chapters describe the life of a high caste Hindu woman and analyse Hindu sacred texts – such as the ‘code of Manu’ – which prescribe the rules for that life. So, statement 2 is correct.
● For her services to the community, the British Raj awarded her the Kaiser-I-Hind medal. Ramabai is also honoured with a feast day on the liturgical calendar of the Episcopal Church (USA) on April 5. The Government of India recognized her contributions to the upliftment of women in India and issued a commemorative stamp on 26th October 1989.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to ‘Singh Sabha Movement’, consider the following statements:
1. It actively opposed imparting modern western education to the Sikhs.
2. The Akali Movement was an offshoot of the Singh Sabha Movement.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Singh Sabha Movement was founded at Amritsar in 1873 with a two-fold objective—to make available modern western education to the Sikhs. For this objective, a network of Khalsa schools was established by the Sabha throughout Punjab. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● It was opposed to the proselytising activities of Christian missionaries as well as the Brahmo Samajists, Arya Samajists and Muslim maulvis. In this direction, everything that went against the Gurus’ teachings was rejected, and rites and customs considered to be consistent with Sikh doctrine were sought to be established.
● The Akali movement (also known as Gurudwara Reform Movement) was an offshoot of the Singh Sabha Movement. It aimed at liberating the Sikh gurudwaras from the control of corrupt Udasi mahants (the post had become hereditary). The government passed the Sikh Gurudwaras Act in 1922 (amended in 1925) which gave the control of gurudwaras to the Sikh masses to be administered through Shiromani Gurudwara Prabandhak Committee (SGPC) as the apex body. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
In the context of the Social reform Movements in British India, consider the following movements:
1. Brahmo Samaj
2. Arya Samaj
3. Aligarh Movement
How many of the above are classified as revivalist movements?
explanation:
● Revivalist movements: They relied to a greater degree on the lost purity of religion they sought to reform.
○ The Arya Samaj was founded in Bombay in 1875 by Swami Dayananda Saraswati, it was a religious revivalist movement that sought to revive the religious beliefs of the Indian people. So, point 2 is correct.
● Reformist movements: They relied more on reason and rationalism in accepting or rejecting a social custom or religious tradition. Following are some reformist movements:
○ Brahmo Samaj: Raja Rammohan Roy founded the Brahmo Sabha in August 1828; it was later renamed Brahmo Samaj. Through the Sabha, he wanted to institutionalise his ideas and mission. The Samaj was committed to “the worship and adoration of the Eternal, Unsearchable, Immutable Being who is the Author and Preserver of the Universe”. So, point 1 is not correct.
○ The Aligarh Movement: It emerged as a liberal, modern trend among the Muslim intelligentsia based in Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College, Aligarh. It aimed at spreading modern education among Indian Muslims without weakening their allegiance to Islam and social reforms among Muslims relating to purdah, polygamy, widow remarriage, women’s education, slavery, divorce, etc. So, point 3 is not correct.
So, only one of the above is classified as revivalist movement.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following social reformers:
1. Margaret Cousins
2. Savitribai Phule
3. Sarojini Naidu
4. Lady Dorab Tata
How many of the above were associated with the All India Women’s Conference, 1927?
explanation:
● The All India Women’s Conference (AIWC) was founded by Margaret Cousins in 1927. It was perhaps the first women’s organisation with an egalitarian approach. Important founding members included Maharani Chimnabai Gaekwad, Rani Sahiba of Sangli, Sarojini Naidu, Kamla Devi Chattopadhyaya and Lady Dorab Tata. So, points 1, 3 and 4 are correct.
● Its objectives were to work for a society based on principles of social justice, integrity, equal rights and opportunities. For this purpose, the AIWC worked towards various legislative reforms before and after India’s independence, some examples being Sarda Act (1929), Hindu Women’s Right to Property Act (1937), Factory Act (1947), Hindu Marriage and Divorce Act (1954), Special Marriage Act (1954), etc.
● Savitribai Phule (1831-1897) was the first female teacher, a modern feminist, and a social reformer of India. Along with her husband Jyotirao Phule, she worked for the upliftment of women in the field of education and other social issues like child marriage, Sati, and dowry systems, etc. She died in 1897. She was not a member of the All India Women Conference. So, point 2 is not correct.
So, only three of the above social reformers were associated with the All India Women's Conference, 1927.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Self-Respect Movement:
1. It was started by Sree Narayana Guru in Kerala.
2. It aimed at complete rejection of the Brahmanical religion and culture.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Self-Respect Movement:
● It was started by E.V. Ramaswamy Naicker, a Balija Naidu, in the mid-1920s. The movement aimed at nothing short of a rejection of the Brahmanical religion and culture which Naicker felt was the prime instrument of exploitation of the lower castes. He sought to undermine the position of brahmin priests by formalizing weddings without brahmin priests. So, statement 1 is not correct and 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Social reforms during the British era, consider the following statements:
1. Meherbai Tata was associated with the National Council of Women.
2. Sarla Devi Chaudhurani founded the Arya Mahila Samaj to serve the cause of women.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● In 1925, the National Council of Women in India, a national branch of the International Council of Women, was formed. Meherbai Tata played a vital role in its formation and advancement. She opined that the purdah system, caste differences and lack of education prevented women from working to solve societal problems. According to critics, the philanthropic-style that was being followed by these women was that of upper-class English women. So, statement 1 is correct.
● In 1910, Sarla Devi Chaudhurani convened the first meeting of the Bharat Stree Mahamandal in Allahabad. So, statement 2 is not correct.
○ Pandita Ramabai Saraswati founded the Arya Mahila Samaj to serve the cause of women. She pleaded for the improvement in the educational syllabus of Indian women before the English Education Commission which was referred to Queen Victoria. This resulted in medical education for women which started in Lady Dufferin College. Later, Ramabai Ranade established a branch of Arya Mahila Samaj in Bombay.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Justice Movement, consider the following statements:
1. It was started in Madras by C.N. Mudaliar, T.M. Nair and P. Tyagaraja.
2. It primarily aimed to secure jobs and representation for the non-brahmins in the legislature.Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Justice Movement:
● This movement in the Madras Presidency was started by C.N. Mudaliar, T.M. Nair and P. Tyagaraja to secure jobs and representation for the non-brahmins in the legislature. In 1917, Madras Presidency Association was formed which demanded separate representation for the lower castes in the legislature. So, both statements 1 and 2 are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following:
1. Opposition to child marriage
2. Abolition of caste discrimination
3. Upliftment of outcastes
4. Improvement in the condition of widows
The Theosophical Society actively advocated how many of the above developments?
explanation:
● Madame H.P. Blavatsky and Colonel M.S. Olcott along with a group of westerners who were inspired by Indian thought and culture, founded the Theosophical Society in New York City, United States in 1875. In 1882, they shifted their headquarters to Adyar, on the outskirts of Madras in India. Annie Beasant was elected its President after the death of Olcott in 1907. The movement became popular under her leadership.
● The society believed that a special relationship could be established between a person’s soul and God through contemplation, prayer, revelation, etc. They also sought to investigate the unexplained laws of nature and the powers latent in man. It accepted the Hindu beliefs in reincarnation and karma and drew inspiration from the philosophy of the Upanishads and Samkhya, Yoga and Vedanta schools of thought.
● The Theosophical Movement came to be allied with the Hindu renaissance. It aimed to work for the universal brotherhood of humanity without distinction of race, creed, sex, caste or colour. It opposed child marriage and advocated the abolition of caste discrimination, upliftment of outcastes and improvement in the condition of widows. So, points 1, 2, 3 and 4 are correct.
So, the theosophical society actively advocated all four of the above developments.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Faraizi Movement aimed at the eradication of social innovations or un-Islamic practices.
2. The Deoband Movement was started by Muhammad Qasim Nanautavi to train religious leaders for the Muslim community.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Faraizi Movement:
● It was founded by Haji Shariatullah in 1819.
● It aimed at the eradication of social innovations or un-Islamic practices and draw their attention to their duties as Muslims. So, statement 1 is correct.
Deoband Movement:
● It was started by Muhammad Qasim Nanautavi (1832–80) and Rashid Ahmad Gangohi (1828–1905) to train religious leaders for the Muslim community.
● It was organised by the orthodox section among the Muslim ulema as a revivalist movement with the twin objectives of propagating pure teachings of the Quran and Hadis among Muslims and keeping alive the spirit of jihad against the foreign rulers. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Social Service League:
1. It was founded to secure for the masses better and reasonable conditions of life and work.
2. ‘Hitavada’ was published in order to promote the ideas of the Social Service League.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● A follower of Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Narayan Malhar Joshi founded the Social Service League in Bombay in 1911. It aimed to secure for the masses better and reasonable conditions of life and work. They organized many schools, libraries, reading rooms, day nurseries and cooperative societies. Their activities also included the poor and illiterate, excursions for slum dwellers, facilities for gymnasia and theatrical performances, sanitary work, medical relief and boys’ clubs and scout corps. Joshi also founded the All India Trade Union Congress (1920). So, statement 1 is correct.
● Gopal Krishna Gokhale (1866-1915), a liberal leader of the Indian National Congress, founded the Servants of India Society in 1905 with the help of M.G. Ranade. The aim of the society was to train national missionaries for the service of India; to promote, by all constitutional means, the true interests of the Indian people; and to prepare a cadre of selfless workers who were to devote their lives to the cause of the country in a religious spirit. In 1911, the Hitavada began to be published to project the views of the society. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct with reference to the teachings of Dayanand Saraswati?
options: a) He preached for a classless and casteless society.
b) He advocated the Aryan religion being the common religion of all.
c) He called for the revival of Vedic times.
d) He believed in the theory of karma and reincarnation.
explanation:
● Dayananda Saraswati or Mulshankar (1824-1883) was the founder of Arya Samaj Movement. The first Arya Samaj unit was formally set up by him at Bombay in 1875 and later the headquarters of the Samaj were established at Lahore. Dayananda’s views were published in his famous work, Satyarth Prakash (The True Exposition).
● His vision of India included a classless and casteless society, a united India (religiously, socially and nationally), and an India free from foreign rule, with Aryan religion being the common religion of all. He took inspiration from the Vedas and considered them to be ‘India’s Rock of Ages’, the infallible and the true original seed of Hinduism. He gave the slogan “Back to the Vedas”. Dayananda’s slogan of ‘Back to the Vedas’ was a call for a revival of Vedic learning and Vedic purity of religion and not a revival of Vedic times. He accepted modernity and displayed a patriotic attitude to national problems.
● Along with his emphasis on Vedic authority, he stressed the significance of individual interpretation of the scriptures and said that every person has the right of access to God. He criticised later Hindu scriptures such as the Puranas and the ignorant priests for perverting Hinduism.
● Dayananda strongly criticised the escapist Hindu belief in maya (illusion) as the running theme of all physical existence and the aim of human life as a struggle to attain moksha (salvation) through escape from this evil world to seek union with God. Instead, he advocated that God, soul and matter (prakriti) were distinct and eternal entities and every individual had to work out his own salvation in the light of the eternal principles governing human conduct. Thus he attacked the prevalent popular belief that every individual contributed and got back from the society according the principles of niyati (destiny). Dayananda believed in the theory of karma and reincarnation. But he also said the good deeds should be primarily for the good of others and not for self.
● Dayananda launched a frontal attack on Hindu orthodoxy, caste rigidities, untouchability, idolatry, polytheism, belief in magic, charms and animal sacrifices, taboo on sea voyages, feeding the dead through shraddhas, etc.
● Dayananda subscribed to the Vedic notion of chaturvarna system in which a person was identified as a brahmin, kshatriya, vaishya or shudra not by birth but according to the occupation and merit of the person.
Therefore, option (c) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the social reform movements:
1. Seva Sadan was founded by Behramji M. Malabari to spread awareness against child marriage and promote widow remarriage among Hindus.
2. Lex Loci Act, 1850 provided the right to inherit the ancestral property of a person who converted to Christianity.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● ‘Seva Sadan’ was founded by Behramji M. Malabari in 1908 to spread awareness against child marriage and promote widow remarriage among Hindus. The efforts of Malabari led to the passage of the Age of Consent Act in 1891, regulating the age of consent for females. Seva Sadan provided shelter to those women who were exploited and then discarded by society. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Lex Loci Act, 1850 provided the right to inherit the ancestral property of a person who converted to Christianity. The Hindu and Muslim law provided that on conversion, the Hindu's or Muslim's property would be forfeited. The Act changed the Hindu Law of Property by allowing a convert from Hinduism to another religion to inherit the property of his father. It was believed that the Act would open floodgates to Christian conversion. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Tattvabodhini Sabha:
1. It was established to carry on Raja Ram Mohan Roy’s ideals independent of the Brahma Samaj.
2. It aimed at counteracting the rapid progress of Christianity in India and advocated the development of Vedantism.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Debendranath Tagore, established the Tattvabodhini Sabha in 1839 to carry on Raja Ram Mohan Roy's ideals independent of the Brahma Samaj. It aimed at counteracting the rapid progress of Christianity in India and advocated the development of Vedantism. Under the aegis of the Tattvabodhini Sabha, emphasis on indigenous language and culture became much more pronounced. Bengali texts in all subjects were published. A Tattvabodhini Press was established and in 1843 the Tattvabodhini Patrika, a journal of the organization was started for the propagation of ideas. So, both statements 1 and 2 are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with reference to the Indian Social Conference?
1. It met annually from its first session in Madras in 1887 at the same time and venue as the Indian National Congress.
2. The conference advocated inter-caste marriages, opposed polygamy and kulinism.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
● The Indian Social Conference launched the ‘Pledge Movement’ to inspire people to take a pledge against child marriage.
● It was founded by M.G. Ranade and Raghunath Rao. It met annually from its first session in Madras in 1887 at the same time and venue as the Indian National Congress. So, statement 1 is correct.
● The conference advocated inter-caste marriages, opposed polygamy and kulinism. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with reference to the Sikh reform movements?
1. The Namdhari Guru Baba Ram Singh extended ritual equality to women and favoured widow remarriage.
2. Nirankaris advocated remarriage of widows and preached that women should not be treated as unclean at the time of childbirth.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
● The Sikh reform movements—Nirankari, Namdhari and Singh Sabha—underlined the importance of women's participation in their programmes.
● The Namdhari Guru Baba Ram Singh extended ritual equality to women and favoured widow remarriage and condemned the evils of female infanticide and child marriage. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Nirankaris advocated remarriage of widows and preached that women should not be treated as unclean at the time of childbirth. So, statement 2 is correct.
● The Singh Sabha tried to tackle the problem of women through the spread of education among girls and women.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Organisation Associated Leaders
1. Paramahansa Mandali : Ananda Mohan Bose and Umesh Chandra Dutta
2. Sadharan Brahmo Samaj : Atmaram Panduranga and Mahadev Govind Ranade
3. Prarthana Samaj : Durgaram Mehtaji and Dadoba Pandurang
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
● Paramahansa Mandali was a secret socio-religious group, established in 1849, in Bombay and is closely related to Manav Dharma Sabha which was founded in 1844 in Surat. It was started by Durgaram Mehtaji, Dadoba Pandurang and a group of his friends. Dadoba Pandurang assumed leadership of this organisation after he left Manav Dharma Sabha. He outlined his principles in Dharma Vivechan in 1848 for Manav Dharma Sabha and "Paramhansik Bramhyadharma" for Paramahansa Mandali. So, pair 1 is not correctly matched.
● Sadharan Brahmo Samaj was a division of Brahmoism formed as a result of schisms in the Brahmo Samaj in 1866 and 1878 respectively. It was formed in a public meeting of Brahmos held in the Town Hall of Calcutta in 1878. Ananda Mohan Bose, Umesh Chandra Dutta and Sivnath Sastri were the founders of this organisation. So, pair 2 is not correctly matched.
● Prarthana Samaj, a leading society for socio-religious reform, was established in 1867 in Bombay by Atmaram Panduranga. The movement gained momentum and popularity after scholar and reformer Mahadev Govind Ranade joined it. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
So, none of the above pairs are correctly matched.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Santhal Rebellion:
1. It was led by Raja Parahat against the practices of the local moneylenders.
2. A separate Santhal Pargana was created after the revolt to reconcile the local people.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Santhal Rebellion by the Santhals was led by Sidhu and Kanhu (1855- 56) against the practices of zamindars and moneylenders; the rebellion later turned anti-British and was suppressed. Under Sidhu and Kanhu, two brothers, the Santhals proclaimed an end to Company rule and declared the area between Bhagalpur and Rajmahal as autonomous. A desperate attempt was made to restore their lost lands by throwing out the outsiders. They rose in open rebellion against the three prime oppressors—zamindars, mahajans and the government. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● It was after the Santhal Revolt (1855-56) that the Santhal Pargana was created, carving out 5,500 square miles from the districts of Bhagalpur and Birbhum. The colonial state hoped that by creating a new territory for the Santhals and imposing some special laws within it, the Santhals could be conciliated. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Pabna Agrarian Movement, consider the following statements:
1. The enhanced rents beyond legal limits was the primary cause for the movement.
2. The Indian Association under Surendranath Banerjea actively supported the movement
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Pabna peasant uprising originated in the Yusufshahi pargana of Eastern Bengal. The introduction of jute as a cash crop played an important role in the Pabna uprising. The jute economy led to the rise of a new rural middle class of ryots who could assert themselves against the landlords. There was a slump in jute prices from 1873. But the zamindars were not prepared to recognise the distress of the peasants and reduce their demands. The zamindars resorted to enhanced rents beyond legal limits and prevented the tenants from acquiring occupancy rights. So, statement 1 is correct.
● A number of young Indian intellectuals supported the peasants’ cause during the resistance. These included Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, R.C. Dutt and the Indian Association under Surendranath Banerjea. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl.No. Tribal uprising Region
1. Kondh uprisings : Chhattisgarh
2. Kol Uprisings : Chotanagpur
3. Singphos Rebellion : Andhra Pradesh
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
● Kondh uprisings: From 1837 to 1856, the Khonds of the hilly tracts extending from Orissa to the Srikakulam and Visakhapatnam districts of Andhra Pradesh revolted against Company rule. So, pair 1 is not correctly matched.
● Kol Uprisings: Uprisings by the Kols of Chottanagpur was led by Buddho Bhagat (1831). It was directed against the expansion of British rule on their lands and transfer of their lands to outsiders. So, pair 2 is correctly matched.
● Singphos Rebellion: The rebellion of the Singphos in Assam in early 1830 was immediately quelled but they continued to organize revolts. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Indigo revolt of 1860, consider the following statements:
1. It was led by Digambar and Bishnu Biswas against terms imposed by European indigo planters.
2. Dinabandhu Mitra’s Nil Darpan (Indigo Mirror) portrayed the impact of indigo planters on the lives of indigo cultivators.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Indigo Revolt (1859-1860) was a revolt of Bengal indigo cultivators led by Digambar and Bishnu Biswas of Nadia district against terms imposed by European indigo planters. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Dinabandhu Mitra’s Nil Darpan (Indigo Mirror), published in 1860, lucidly portrayed the havoc caused by indigo planters on the lives of indigo cultivators. It created a huge controversy and was later banned by the East India Company to control farmer agitation. It was translated into English by Michael Madhusudan Dutt. So, statement 2 is correct.
● The Government appointed an Indigo Commission (1860) to inquire into the problem of indigo cultivation. Based on its recommendations, the Government issued a notification in November 1860 that the ryots could not be compelled to grow indigo and that it would ensure that all disputes were settled by legal means. It did not recommend cash compensation to indigo cultivators.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the revolt of dewan Velu Thampi:
1. It was launched against the East India Company’s harsh conditions imposed on Mysore kingdom.
2. Kundara Proclamation which called for taking up arms against the British was associated with the revolt.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The East India Company’s harsh conditions imposed on the state of Travancore, after both of them agreed to a subsidiary alliance arrangement under Wellesley in 1805, caused deep resentment in the region. The ruler was not able to pay the subsidy and fell in arrears. The British resident of Travancore was meddling in the internal affairs of the state. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● The highhanded attitude of the Company compelled Prime Minister (or Dalawa) Velu Thampi to rise against the Company, assisted by the Nair troops. Velu Thampi addressed a gathering in Kundara, openly calling for taking up arms against the British to oust them from the native soil. This was later known as the Kundara Proclamation. There was a large-scale rebellion against the British as a result. A large military operation had to be undertaken to restore peace. The Maharaja of Travancore had not wholly supported the rebellion and defected to the side of the Company. Velu Thampi killed himself to avoid capture. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Ramoshi Rebellion in Western India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. It aimed to rid the country of the British by instigating an armed revolt by disrupting communication lines.
2. It led to the assasination of the plague commissioner Walter Charles Rand.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
● The first of the revolutionary activities in Maharashtra was the organisation of the Ramoshi Peasant Force by Vasudev Balwant Phadke in 1879, which aimed to rid the country of the British by instigating an armed revolt by disrupting communication lines. It hoped to raise funds for its activities through dacoities. It was suppressed prematurely. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Chapekar brothers, Damodar and Balkrishna, assassinated Walter Charles Rand, the chairman of the plague committee, in Poona for his overzealous haste and stringent and drastic measures to control plague. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Ahom revolt:
1. It was led under the leadership of Gomdhar Konwar.
2. It resulted in the British handing over Upper Assam to Maharaja Purandar Singh Narendra.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Ahoms became a paramount power in the whole of the Brahmaputra Valley under Sukhangpha (1293-1332).
● The British had pledged to withdraw from Assam after the First Burma War (1824-26). But, after the war, instead of withdrawing, the British attempted to incorporate the Ahoms’ territories in their dominion. This sparked off a rebellion in 1828 under the leadership of Gomdhar Konwar, an Ahom prince, along with compatriots, such as Dhanjoy Bongohain, and Jairam Khargharia Phukan. Assembling near Jorhat, the rebels formally made Gomdhar Konwar the king. Finally, the Company decided to follow a conciliatory policy and handed over Upper Assam to Maharaja Purandar Singh Narendra and part of the kingdom was restored to the Assamese king. So, statements 1 and 2 are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the ‘Fituri Revolt of 1879’, consider the following statements:
1. Its primary cause was the British policy of ban on the practice of religious sacrifice.
2. It was noteworthy for the use of legal means and non violence.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Fituri revolt or the Rampa revolt of 1879 was led by the hill tribes in the Rampa region of the Vizagapatam Hill Tracts Agency of Vizagapatam District against the British government of the Madras Presidency.
○ The revolt occurred because of the government’s ban on the brewing of local liquor which was an important source of nourishment for the people and the granting of leases to traders of the plains to brew palm liquor.
○ The Madras government had introduced a law-making toddy tapping illegal and introducing a toddy tax. Toddy tapping was part of their culture. This led to a full-scale rebellion in early 1879. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● The rebellion started in March 1879 when the hill tribes of Rampa made attacks on police stations in Chodavaram taluk. Already reduced to a position of dependence and subordination, the tribesmen of Rampa hills felt that since they could not live anyway, ‘they might as well kill the constables and die’. They consistently attacked police stations which were a visible symbol of state authority. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the ‘Rangpur Rebellion,’ consider the following statements:
1. It was a peasant movement against the revenue farming system.
2. The government made attempts to pacify the rebels by reducing the rent level of all parganas under the control of revenue farmers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The peasant’s uprising in Rangpur, Bengal, was launched in 1783, under the leadership of a small landholder, Dhiraj Naraya against their exploitation by the revenue farmer Devi Singh. The rebel peasants styled their leader as nawab and stormed the headquarters of the revenue farmer at Kazirhat. The government ruthlessly suppressed the rebellion first, and then took attempts to pacify the rebels by ancelling the contracts of revenue farmers and reducing the rent level of all parganas under his control. So, both statements 1 and 2 are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs with reference to colonial India:
Sl. No. Tribal Leader Region
1. Helen Lepcha : Sikkim
2. Ramji Gond : Odisha
3. Chakra Bisoi : Telangana
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation:
● Helen Lepcha, also known as Savitri Devi, was a prominent freedom fighter from the hills of Darjeeling. She belonged to the Lepcha tribe from present day Sikkim. In 1941, she helped Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose escape from house arrest and travel to Germany. She was also inspired by Gandhi's Charkha Movement. So, pair 1 is correctly matched.
● Ramji Gond was a Gond chief who ruled the tribal areas in present-day Adilabad district of Telangana. He fought against British rule and died in 1860 after being caught and hanged. He is considered a freedom fighter and a hero by many in Telangana. So, pair 2 is not correctly matched.
● Chakra Bisoi was a young Raja who led the Khond people in the Khond Uprising against the British government in Odisha in 1846 and 1855. He was allegedly aided by Somnath Singh, the King of Angul. The Khonds played a vital role in preparing the revolt of 1857 against the British Government. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched.
So, only three pairs are correctly matched.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following statements is correct with reference to the Socio-Religious Reform Movements in India?
options: a) These movements had a broad social base including peasantry and urban poor.
b) The masses totally shifted away from religious scriptures and towards scientific thinking.
c) The nature of these movements contributed to communalism in modern times.
d) These movements failed to establish national consciousness among the masses.
explanation:
● One of the major limitations of the religious reform movements was that they had a narrow social base, namely the educated and urban middle classes, while the needs of the vast masses of peasantry and the urban poor were ignored.
● The tendency of reformers to appeal to the greatness of the past and to rely on scriptural authority encouraged mysticism in new garbs and fostered pseudo-scientific thinking while exercising a check on the full acceptance of the need for a modern scientific outlook. But, above all, these tendencies contributed, at least to some extent, in compartmentalizing Hindus, Muslims, Sikhs, and Parsis, as also alienating high-caste Hindus from low-caste Hindus.
● The underlying concern of these reformist efforts was revival of the native cultural personality which had got distorted by various factors over the years. This cultural ideological struggle was to prove to be an important instrument in the evolution of national consciousness and a part of Indian national resolve to resist colonial cultural and ideological hegemony.
● Overemphasis on religious, philosophical aspects of culture while under emphasized secular and moral aspects Hindus confined their praise to ancient Indian history and Muslims to medieval history created a notion of two separate peoples and increased communal consciousness. Many other factors were certainly responsible for the birth of communalism in modern times, but, undoubtedly, the nature of religious reform movements also contributed to it.
Therefore option (c) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Shuddhi Movement:
1. It was started by the Arya Samaj.
2. The movement resulted in the increased communal consciousness of the masses.
Which of the above given statements is/are correct?
explanation:
● In its zeal to protect the Hindu society from the onslaught of Christianity and Islam, the Arya Samaj started the shuddhi (purification) movement to reconvert to the Hindu fold the converts to Christianity and Islam. So, statement 1 is correct.
● An aggressive campaign of shuddhi led to increasing communalisation of social life during the 1920s and later snowballed into communal political consciousness. The shuddhi movement also attempted to uplift those regarded as untouchables and outside the caste system of Hindus into pure caste Hindus. So, statement 2 is correct.
○ Shuddhi Movement was started by Arya Samaj in early 20th century to bring back those Hindu people back to their religion, who had converted themselves to Islam and Christianity. Dayananda Saraswati claimed to be rejecting all non-Vedic beliefs altogether. Hence the Arya Samaj unequivocally condemned idolatry, animal sacrifices, ancestor worship, pilgrimages, priestcraft, offerings made in temples, the caste system, untouchability and child marriages, on the grounds that all these lacked Vedic sanction. The process of reconverting Hindus, who had embraced Islam known as Shuddhi, was accompanied by a Sanghathan movement, which was launched to unify the Hindus and organize them in self-defense. Literally speaking Shuddhi means purification but when used by the Arya Samajists, it means reconversion.
Therefore option (c) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Baba Dayal Das, consider the following statements:
1. He started the Nirankari movement for reforms in Sikhism.
2. He preached idol worship of human gurus of Sikhism.
3. He introduced a simplified version of marriage named Anand Karaj.
How many of the statements given above is/are not correct?
explanation:
● Nirankar means the Formless One. This message became the foundation of the Nirankari movement. Baba Dayal Das, a contemporary of Maharaja Ranjit Singh, was the first among the reformers in Sikh religion, who condemned the social evils that had gradually crept in Sikh society and started the Nirankari movement. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Under this system, marriage is performed in the presence of the Guru Granth, with the priest singing four relevant hymns from this holy book of the Sikhs. No other rituals are performed and dowry, marriage procession, drinking and dancing are disapproved. He also preached against idol worship of human gurus. So, statement 2 is not correct.
● He disapproved of the worship of tombs and graves. He also introduced a simplified version of marriage named Anand Karaj which later got legal recognition with the passage of the Anand Marriage Act in 1909. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, consider the following statements:
1. He established the United Indian Patriotic Association to oppose the Indian National Congress.
2. He was opposed to the introduction of western scientific education among Muslims.
3. He established a Scientific Society for the translation of literature from English to Urdu.
How many of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● Sir Syed Ahmed was a liberal, modern socio-religious reformer born in 1817 who served the British administration before the revolt of 1857. United Indian Patriotic association was a political organization founded in 1888 by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan and Raja Shiv Prasad Singh of Benares. It opposed the Indian National Congress and aimed to develop closer ties between Muslim community and the British Raj. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Sir Syed Ahmed Khan propagated western scientific education among the Muslim masses. He wanted to reconcile western scientific education with the teachings in the Quran. So, statement 2 is not correct.
● He established the Scientific Society in 1864 to spread western education among Muslims through Urdu translation. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Tayuni Movement in India, consider the following statements:
1. It was started by Haji Shariatullah in 1839 at Dacca.
2. It sought to restore the old pure form of Islam by eliminating innovations and syncretistic practices.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● The Tayuni movement was founded in 1839 by Karamat Ali Jaunpuri at Dacca (Bangladesh). The Faraizi movement was started by Haji Shariatullah. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● The Tayuni movement (1839) is inspired by the religious teachings of Shah Waliullah. It was opposed to Faraizi movement’s view that India under British rule was Dar-ul-Harb where Muslims should not practice their religious rituals. It rejected the new elements which have entered the Islam and aimed to restore the old pure form of Islam by removing innovations and syncretistic practices. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: In the context of Colonial India, Krishna Mohan Banerjee, Mahesh Chandra Ghosh, Ram Gopal Ghosh are remembered as:
options: a) leaders who opposed the Quit India Movement
b) proponents of the Temple entry movement in Guruvayur
c) leaders associated with Young Bengal Movement
d) members of the Civil Disobedience movement in West Bengal
explanation:
● The Young Bengal Movement was started by Henry Vivian Derozio in the early 1830s. It was a radical, intellectual trend among the youth in Bengal drawing inspiration from the French revolution. The main reason for the limited success of the movement is the lack of real link with the masses and their failure to take up peasant’s cause. Derozions supported women’s right to equality and demanded the education of women. Krishna Mohan Banerjee, Mahesh Chandra Ghosh, Ram Gopal Ghosh and Dakshinarajan Mukherjee were prominent leaders associated with the movement.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Child Marriage Restraint Act, 1929:
1. It set the legal marriage age for both boys and girls at 15 years.
2. Harbilas Sarda, the Member of the Central Legislative Assembly introduced a Bill for the enactment of the Act.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
● With the growth of women’s organizations and writings on these issues, the momentum for reform gained strength. People challenged some of the established custom, like that of child marriage. According to the Child Marriage Restraint Act, 1929 no man below the age of 18 and woman below the age of 14 could marry. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● Harbilas Sarda was an Indian academic and judge, who became a Member of the Central Legislative Assembly from Ajmer-Merwara. He was a follower of Dayanand Saraswati and a member of the Arya Samaj. He was the one whointroduced a Bill for the enactment of the Act. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Who among the following were associated with the Women’s Indian Association (WIA)?
1. Annie Besant
2. Margaret Cousins
3. Sarojini Naidu
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
The movement for women’s suffrage began in the early 1900s in India. Following are the important events:
● 1917: The Women’s Indian Association (WIA), with links to the women’s suffrage movement, was formed by Annie Besant, Dorothy Jinrajadasa and Margaret Cousins. It was formed in Madras to create a vehicle for women to influence government policy. So, points 1 and 2 are correct.
● 1918: The Montagu-Chelmsford Commission was sent by the British Government to suggest changes to the franchise in India where the WIA, led by Sarojini Naidu, petitioned for women’s right to vote. So, point 3 is correct.
● 1918: The Southborough Franchise Committee toured India to gather information. After accepting women’s petitions from just two provinces, it decided Indian women do not want the right to vote
● 1918-21: After dogged lobbying with the Joint Select Committee, the Parliament decided to leave the issue to be settled by elected legislatures.
● 1921: The Madras Presidency voted to remove the restriction on standing for elections at the local level, striking the sex qualification for women. Therefore, Madras is among the first States to give women the right to vote. M.C. Devadoss was the first woman elected to the Madras City Corporation in the 1921 election.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Which of the following factors were responsible for the rise of socio-religious reform movements in India?
1. The practice of Kulinism
2. The influence of western culture
3. The overwhelming influence of the priests
4. Lack of upward social mobility
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
● The dawn of the 19th century witnessed the birth of a new vision, a modern vision among some enlightened sections of Indian society. This enlightened vision was to shape the course of events for decades to come and even beyond. Various factors responsible for the rise of the socio-religious reform movement in India are as follows:
● Depressing Position of Women: The practice of polygamy prevailed, and in Bengal, under Kulinism, even old men took very young girls as wives. Kulinism is derived from the Sanskrit word kulina, which means a good family. Raja Vallala Sena of Bengal popularised kulinism. Brides from lower castes married grooms from the top three castes in Hinduism. Several women hardly had a married life worth the name, yet when their husbands died, they were expected to commit sati. So, point 1 is correct.
● Opposition to the Western Culture: The establishment of colonial rule in India was followed by a systematic attempt to disseminate colonial culture and ideology as the dominant cultural current. Faced with the challenge of the intrusion of colonial culture and ideology, an attempt to reinvigorate traditional institutions and to realize the potential of traditional culture developed during the 19th century. So, point 2 is correct.
● Religious and Social Ills: Indian society in the 19th century was caught in a vicious web created by religious superstitions and social obscurantism. Hinduism had become steeped in magic and superstition. The priests exercised an overwhelming and, indeed, unhealthy influence on the minds of the people. Idolatry and polytheism helped to reinforce their position, and their monopoly of scriptural knowledge imparted a deceptive character to all religious systems. There was nothing that religious ideology could not persuade people to do. So, point 3 is correct.
● The Caste Problem: Another debilitating factor was caste. This entailed a system of segregation, hierarchically ordained on the basis of ritual status. Under a rigid caste system, social mobility was checked, social divisions grew, and individual initiative was thwarted. Above all, the humiliation of untouchability so much a part of the caste system militates against human dignity. So, point 4 is correct.
Therefore option (d) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText: He was a pioneering Indian social reformer working for women’s emancipation. His place in Indian history as a reformer is due to the Maharaj libel case in 1862 which earned him the title of “a Reformer, a Martin Luther of the Banian Cast”. In 1852, he founded the Satya Prakash in Gujarati to promote widow remarriage. He was:
explanation:
● Karsandas Mulji was a contemporary of Dadabhai Naoroji. He was a pioneering Indian social reformer working for women's emancipation. Mulji was a prominent member of the Bombay intelligentsia in conflict with the merchant aristocracy over social issues in the 1850s and 60s.
● Mulji's place in Indian history as a reformer is due to the Maharaj libel case in 1862 which earned him the title of "a Reformer, a Martin Luther of the Banian Cast". In 1852, he founded the Satya Prakash in Gujarati to promote widow remarriage.
● Moreover, all the other personalities: B.M. Malabari, D.K. Karve, and Vishnu Shastri Pandit were also associated with their efforts to promote widow remarriage.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History
